coredata.dc:title: One-pot green synthesis of carbon dots from Swietenia macrophylla for fluorescent Fe3+ ion sensing and catalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Indian Chemical Society
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Indian Chemical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Selvaraj, Hosimin
Kalainilavan, Kumaresan
Sri, Kaleeswaran Sneka
George, Hema Sindhuja
Aravind, Priyadharshini
Thangavel, Kavitha
Ilangovan, Andivelu
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we have synthesised carbon dots using microwave-assisted methods. Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots (SMCDs) were decorated with oxygenated functional groups confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy. Carbon dots emit fluorescence peaks at 388 nm with an excitation wavelength was about 325 nm, and the quantum yield of the carbon dots was 9.56 %. The water-soluble carbon dots have good quenching efficiency with ferric ions and act as fluorescence-selective sensors in an aqueous medium. The ferric ion detection limit was 2.3 μM. Synthesised carbon dots also possessed a good catalytic reducing activity with methylene blue dye (92 % reduction). Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots are used to detect the ferric ion in an aqueous medium and acts as a reducing catalyst in the methylene blue reduction process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microwave
Emission
Seed
Methylene blue
Reduction

Kothari postdoctoral fellowship (DSKPDF) & School of Chemistry, Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli, Tamil Nadu, India, Prof. Andivelu Ilangovan, Kumaresan Kalainilavan and Kaleeswaran Sneka Sri thanks RUSA 2.0 (No.51788/P3/RUSA/2021) for providing funding and facilities at Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620024, Tamil Nadu, India, Dr. Hema Sindhuja George acknowledges the management of Jamal Mohamed College, Tiruchirappalli 620020, Tamil Nadu. India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of SMCDs.

a) UV–Visible absorption spectra for Swietenia macrophylla Carbon Dots (inside the picture carbon dot in the presence of UV light @365 nm and daylight). b) FT-IR spectra of Swietenia Macrophylla seed raw powder and Swietenia macrophylla derived Carbon Dots (SMCDs).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra for Swietenia macrophylla Carbon dots with different excitation wavelengths (b) Fluorescence emission wavelength of Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots with different excitation wavelength. (c) Stability of carbon dots with Irradiation by UV light at 365 nm and (d) stability at various pH.
a) and b) HRTEM image of the SMCDs, c) Average particle size of SMCDs, d) XRD spectra of SMCDs.
a) Fluorescence emission spectra for Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots with the addition of various metal ions. b) F/F0 of Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots with various metal solutions.

a) Fluorescence spectra of SMCDs with different concentrations of Fe3+with excitation wavelength 325 nm. b) Various Fe3+ion concentration vs Fluorescence intensity (linear detection range R2 value is 0.9950).
a) Methylene blue reaction with NaBH4 b) Methylene blue reaction with NaBH4+SMCDs c) Reduction of MB, d) linear transform ln (C0/C) of the kinetic curves of MB using SMCDs as a catalyst.

head.ce:title: One-pot green synthesis of carbon dots from Swietenia macrophylla for fluorescent Fe3+ ion sensing and catalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli, Tamil Nadu, 620024, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli, Tamil Nadu- 620024, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering and Technology, Dhanalaksmi Srinivasan University, Perambalur-621212, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Jamal Mohamed College, Tiruchirappalli, Tamil Nadu, 620020, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology, Alagappa University, Karaikudi, 630003, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microbiology, Alagappa University, Karaikudi-630003 Tamil Nadu- India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry, Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli, Tamil Nadu, 620024, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we have synthesised carbon dots using microwave-assisted methods. Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots (SMCDs) were decorated with oxygenated functional groups confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy. Carbon dots emit fluorescence peaks at 388 nm with an excitation wavelength was about 325 nm, and the quantum yield of the carbon dots was 9.56 %. The water-soluble carbon dots have good quenching efficiency with ferric ions and act as fluorescence-selective sensors in an aqueous medium. The ferric ion detection limit was 2.3 μM. Synthesised carbon dots also possessed a good catalytic reducing activity with methylene blue dye (92 % reduction). Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots are used to detect the ferric ion in an aqueous medium and acts as a reducing catalyst in the methylene blue reduction process.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are fascinating and swiftly up-warding in the area of nanotechnology which gets more attention due to their unique properties such as biocompatibility, optical properties and sustainability [1,2]. Carbon dots are spherical and possess sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms with the high surface area and <10 nm size of the particles. Carbon dots are synthesised from various biomass/biowastes such as plant seeds, fruit, flowers etc obeying the principles of green chemistry [3–5]. The eco-friendly method helps to convert waste into useful carbon materials, which are used in various applications such as energy storage devices, sensors, bioimages [6], antibacterial agents [7], photocatalysts [8], etc. Carbon dots are decorated by oxygen-containing functional groups such as carbonyl (C=O) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups which participate in the chemical properties of carbon dots [9]. Carbon dots have unique properties such as photostability, water solubility, nontoxicity, biocompatibility and ease of functionalization etc[10–13]. The carbon dots synthesis route is classified into Bottom-up and Top-down methods. In bottom-up methods, small aromatic molecules form carbon dots and in top-down approach, the disintegration of carbonaceous materials converts into carbon dots [11,14,15].

Ferric ions play an important role in various physiological activities such as oxygen/electron transfer and are involved in biological reduction/oxidation processes. Ferric ions are essential trace elements involves the respiration of biological systems [16,17]. If Ferric ions are not balanced in our body it leads to the creation of disease in our body. If Fe3+ ion concentration increases it affects internal organs such as the heart, pancreas, liver, kidney, etc. So Fe3+ ion balancing is more important in our body. Fe3+ ions also affect the environment, and monitoring of Fe3+ ions is very important [18–20], a lot of methods are available to detect the Fe3+ metal ions such as electrochemical method, mass spectrometric, atomic fluorescence spectrometry and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS). These techniques are high-cost as well as use harmful organic solvents which cause some environmental problems [21,22]. So, need to develop a cost-effective method and avoid to use of harmful organic solvents. Fluorescence detection methods. The fluorescence detection approach is adopted for Fe3+ ions sensing techniques which is also cost-effective compared to other methods.

The development of textile industries generates more amount of toxic dyeing wastewater which directly affects the water bodies, living organisms and the environment [23–30]. Cationic dyes of methylene blue are widely used in textile industries for the dyeing process which affects the water body of living organisms, so suitable treatment methods are needed to reduce the methylene blue in the textile industries wastewater [31,32]. Photooxidation/photoreduction technology is adopted to treat the wastewater due to low energy consumption [33]. Carbon dots can be used as catalysts to reduce the methylene blue dyes. CDs can act as photosensitizers under visible light, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide anions (O2 •−), which can degrade organic pollutants [34,35]. N and S doped carbon dots are used as photocatalysts for the methylene blue reduction process [36]. (S. Hu, R. Tian). Undoped carbon dots also reduced the methylene blue effectively [37].

In the present work, fluorescence carbon dots were synthesised by simple, economical and one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis. Swietenia macrophylla was used as a carbon source for the carbon dots which have good water solubility properties. It can be used for various application processes. Synthesised carbon dots effectively enhance the rate of the reducing reaction of the methylene blue dye and also used as fluorescence probe sensing of transition metal ions due to good quenching behaviour with Fe3+ ions. Carbon dots show good selectivity and sensitivity of Fe3+ ions, so it act as fluorescence sensor probes. The fluorescence detection process is a very simple and cost-effective method compared to other sophisticated methods. Overall, synthesised carbon dots potentially play an important role in the field of environmental remediation.

Analytical grade chemicals MgCl2, CaCl2 PbCl2, CuCl2 ZnCl2 BaCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, NiCl2 and CoCl2, were purchased from SRL chemicals. Ethanol was purchased from CSC. Methylene blue and Quinine sulfate (98 %) and 85 % H3PO4 were obtained from Merck (India), and biological compounds from Merck (India). All Chemicals were used as received. Preparation of standard salt solution and for dilution purposes, double-distilled water has been used in entire experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave carbonization of Swietenia macrophylla seeds
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots were prepared using a microwave-assisted carbonization process from Swietenia macrophylla seed. The synthesis procedure was followed by George. et al. [22]. The procedure briefly: 1g dried Swietenia macrophylla was added into a 500 mL beaker containing 10 ml of orthophosphoric acid and mixed well till the residual slurry obtained. The slurry was carbonized using a 700W domestic microwave oven (LG, Model: MS2043DB) for 90 sec. After carbonization, a dark brown residue obtained was cooled at room temperature. The residue was dispersed with 30 mL of ethanol and mechanically stirred for 10 min. The ethanolic dispersed medium was filtered using Whatman filter paper and obtained brownish carbon dots solutions, which were dried at 90ᵒC. 10 ml of double distilled water was added to the dried powder and collected as water soluble carbon dots. The brown coloured solution was filtered using a 0.25 μm syringe filter and named Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots (SMCDs). Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram of the synthesis of Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots.

The size and morphologies of the carbon dots were studied using High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2100 plus). The nature of the carbon dots was analysed using an X-ray diffraction (XRD) study (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). Absorption spectra were recorded using UV-1800 SHIMADZU spectrometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by JASCO FP-8300 spectrometer. Fourier Transform Infra-Red Spectra (FTIR) were recorded using JASCO FT/IR-6600, an instrument ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1. From the HRTEM data, the average size of the particles was calculated using ImageJ software and the photographic image was taken by a Moto e40 mobile phone.

The quantum yield of the Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots was calculated using the relative method, wherein quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as a solvent, Qs = 0.546) as a reference with an excitation wavelength was 325 nm.
Qs =
Q R
I S
I R
X
η s 2
η R 2

Qs is the quantum yield of the carbon dots), QR quantum yield of quinine sulfate (0.546), I is integrated areas of the fluorescence spectrum of carbon dots and the reference respectively. All samples intensity absorbance values should be less than 0.1, to avoid the absorbance effect. η
s and η
R were refractive induction of the solvent and reference, respectively.

Catalytic property of synthesised carbon dots was examined using UV–visible spectrometer method to reduce methylene blue (MB) solution using sodium borohydride (NaBH4). Here, the two different processes were conducted, with and without Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots in a mixture of NaBH4 and MB. 2.5 mL of methylene blue solution (3 g/L) was taken into a 3.5 mL quartz cuvette and added freshly prepared 0.5 mL of NaBH4 (0.01 M) [38]. UV–Vis spectrum was recorded in the mixture for every 1-minute interval. A similar procedure was followed with 0.25 mL of synthesised carbon dots. The experiment was repeated in triplicate.

Catalytic efficiency is calculated using the following equation
Catalytic
efficiency
( % )
=
C i
−
C f
C i
X
100
Where Ci is the initial and Cf final concentration(absorbance) of Methylene blue.

10 mmol concentration of various metal chloride solutions were freshly prepared using double distilled water. 1 mL of metal solution, 1 mL of distilled water and 1 mL of synthesised carbon dots solution were mixed into a 5 mL glass vial which was kept under the UV light @365 nm and observed the fluorescence response of the various metal solutions. The same solutions were examined for the fluorescence response with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The quenching behaviour of synthesised carbon dots was observed with varying concentrations (0–50 μmol) of Ferric ion. Stern –Volmer Equation explains the static quenching behaviour of synthesised carbon dos [39].
(2) F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q]
Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of metal ion solution. Ksv-stern volmer quenching constant and [Q] is metal ion concentration. The SMCDs metal ion limit of detection (LOD) was estimated using the following formula [Hema et al., 2023 ]
(3) LOD = 3σ/s
Where s is the slope of the calibration plot and σ is the standard deviation of the signals.

The photostability of carbon dots was evaluated using fluorescence spectra. Two different light sources were used to evaluate the photostability of the synthesised carbon dots which were kept at room temperature for 60 days and recorded fluorescence spectra at various time intervals. The carbon dots solution was kept under UV–visible light (365 nm) for 60 min and the fluorescence spectra were recorded for every 10 min. The carbon dots solution was adjusted with different pH like 3,5,7,9,11 and 13 and also recorded fluorescence spectra with 325 nm excitation wavelength to reveal the photostable nature of SMCDs.

Biomass-derived carbon dots were synthesised from Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots by the microwave carbonation process. Synthesised carbon dots have good solubility and optical properties in the water medium. Fig. 1 shows the schematic view of carbon dots synthesis. Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots show the blue colour emission @ 365 nm UV light and light brown colour at normal visible light. Fig. 2 a shows the UV spectrum of the carbon dots with two broad absorption peaks @ 275 nm and 325 nm, which correspond to π-π∗ transition of the C=C bond and n-π∗ transition for C=O. Based on the UV–visible spectra to record fluorescence spectra, the Wavelength has been adjusted to record the fluorescence spectra for SMCDs, the exact excitation and emission peaks. Fig. 2b shows the FTIR spectra for Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots. Peaks at 2925 cm−1 and 2855 cm−1 in the IR spectrum of raw Swietenia macrophylla seed powder represent the stretching frequency of sp3(C–H) and sp2 (C–H) groups. However, in carbon dots, these peaks disappeared and abroad new broad peak appearing at 3364 cm−1 indicate the presence of O–H vibration, 2916 cm−1 corresponds to C–H stretching vibration, carbonyl (C=O) functional group shows at 1737 cm−1,1363 cm−1 indicates the C–H stretching vibration and C–O bond at 1216 cm−1 vibrational frequency. The FT-IR spectra predict that the synthesised Swietenia macrophylla Carbon Dots were decorated with –COOH and OH groups. Fig. 3 a shows the fluorescence spectra of Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 315 to 385 nm. At 325 nm excitation wavelength SMCDs show high-intensity spectra which correspond to 388 nm emission wavelength. The emission peaks gradually move from 385 to 445 nm, which denotes the redshift. Fig. 3b show the excitation and emission spectra of the Swietenia macrophylla. The photostability of the carbon dots was examined by irradiating with UV–Visible light for 60 min and recording the fluorescence spectra. Fig. 3c shows the fluorescence intensity of the synthesised carbon dots with good photostability in the presence of UV–visible light. Fig. 3d shows the stability of the carbon dots with varying pH medium, and in neutral pH the SMCDs show good fluorescence emission intensity peak.

Fig. 4 a and b shows that the HRTEM image of the Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots displays dot-like structures. The average size of the SMCDs was about 4.8 nm (Fig. 4c), and the lattice space range was 0.238 nm with monodispersed spherical shapes. Fig. 4 d. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CDs showed a single broad peak centred at 2θ = 23.58°, The calculated d-spacing of about 3.76 Å is larger than that of pure graphite (3.35 Å), which indicates that the material is disordered carbon and corresponds to the (002) plane with some structural distortion.

Synthesised carbon dots have excellent optical properties decorated by carboxylic acid and hydroxy functional groups which plays a vital role in quenching ability with other metals or compounds. To examine the quenching behaviour of Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots with different metal solutions (1 mmol concentration) such as MgCl2, CaCl2 PbCl2, CuCl2 ZnCl2 BaCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, NiCl2 and CoCl2. Freshly prepared metal solutions were mixed with Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots and each metal solution was recorded with fluorescence spectra, the fluorescence intensity response at 330 nm excitation wavelength is shown in Fig. 5 a. Among all carbon dots mixed metal ions solutions, Fe3+ ions and Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots mixed solutions do not show the same level of intensity peaks and form this observation it is stated that the carbon dots were effectively quenched with Fe3+ ions and other metal ions show nearly the same intensity. The reason is, the Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots have strong interaction of the surface functional groups (carboxylic and hydroxy) with the Fe3+ ion vacant d-orbitals known as electronic interactions.

SMCDs shows a strong fluorescence nature, which has a unique electronic structure with oxygen-containing surface functional groups such as –OH and –COOH. When Fe3+ ions are introduced into a solution containing SMCDs, these ions interact strongly with the electron-rich functional groups on the SMCDs surface through coordination or electrostatic interactions. This interaction disrupts the normal excited-state processes within the SMCDs, leading to fluorescence quenching. One major mechanism involved is static quenching, where a non-fluorescent complex forms between Fe3+ and the SMCDs, preventing fluorescence emission. Additionally, dynamic quenching may occur through collisional interactions that promote non-radiative decay of the excited-state electrons. Another key pathway is photoinduced electron transfer (PET), where excited electrons in the CDs are transferred to the Fe3+ ions, which are strong electron acceptors. This transfer reduces radiative recombination, thereby quenching the fluorescence. The high charge density and redox potential of Fe3+ enhance its ability to withdraw electrons from the SMCDs, promoting energy loss through non-radiative pathways. In some systems, Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) might also contribute if spectral overlap conditions are met. However, PET and static quenching are generally the dominant mechanisms in SMCD-based Fe3+ detection. The strong and specific interaction between Fe3+ and surface groups on SMCDs ensures high quenching efficiency, which makes SMCDs highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probes for Fe3+ ions. Other metal ions typically do not induce similar quenching effects, due to weaker interactions or lower redox activity, highlighting the selectivity of this sensing system.

Fig. 5b shows the bar diagram of the various metal ions with the same concentration. Stern-Volmer equation explains the static quenching behaviour of synthesised carbon dots. Fluorescence intensity was measured for all metal ion solutions. F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the Swietenia macrophyllacarbon dots, and F represents the fluorescence intensity of the metal ion solution. F/F0 value is the same for Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots and all metal ion solutions, except Fe3+ ion solution, which indicates that newly synthesised carbon dots possess effective quenching with Fe3+ ion. Hence, Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots can be used as fluorescence sensor probes for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions.

Fig. 6 a shows the fluorescence spectra for different concentrations of Fe3+ ions with SMCDs. 388 nm Emission peaks intensity gradually decreased with increasing concentration of the Fe3+ ion from 10 μmol to 50 μmol, denoting that Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots were effectively quenched with Fe3+ ion with various concentrations. Fig. 6b shows a good linear regression between the quenching ratio vs different concentrations of Fe3+ ion solutions. The limit of detection was about 2.45 μmol. Swietenia macrophylla carbon dots were able to detect and were highly sensitive to the low concentration of Fe3+ ions in an aqueous medium.

Methylene blue is more toxic, non-biodegradable, and carcinogenic, creating serious problems for both the environment and human beings. MB is used in the dyeing process in textile industries, which is discharged as effluent and affects the environment. The catalytic reduction process is one of the best short-time processes. Although NaBH4 is a good reducing agent for various organic synthesis processes, it cannot reduce certain organic compounds on its own. In such cases, a catalyst is required to facilitate the reduction process. Here, synthesised carbon dots are used as a catalyst to enhance the reduction behaviour of the NaBH4 in the MB dye reduction process. MB absorption wavelength was 665 nm during the catalytic process absorbance intensity was monitored at different time intervals using a UV–visible spectrometer. Fig. 7 a shows the spectrum of reduction behaviour of NaBH4 without carbon dots. MB intensity absorbance peak was reduced slightly up to 120 min which means 10 % reduction was achieved without carbon dots. Fig. 7b shows the absorbance spectrum of the reduction of MB dye with NaBH4 and carbon dots. MB intensity gradually decreased within 16 min, which denoted that a small amount of carbon dots act as a catalyst and enhance the reduction rate of the NaBH4 with 92% of reduction. The Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism explains the catalytic behaviour of a reducing agent with a catalyst. Dye molecules and the reducing agent of the NaBH4 interaction mechanism are a charge transfer mechanism because NaBH4 serves as an electron donor, and dye molecules act as electron acceptors. In the absence of a catalyst, the amount of electron transfer between borohydride (BH4 −) and dye molecules was less, so the reduction rate was very slow. while adding carbon dots (catalyst) reduction rate was increased. The carbon dots were absorbed on the dye surface and acted as mediators between BH4 − and dye molecules. Carbon dots enhance the electron transfer rate so the MB dye reduction time was decreased compared to the absence of carbon dots. Methylene blue was reduced and converted into colourless Leuco methylene blue (LMB). Fig. 7c shows the reduction behaviour of methylene blue in the presence of Swietenia Macrophylla carbon dots catalyst. The reaction kinetics plot was plotted between ln (Ct/C0) and Time (min) for methylene blue dye. Fig. 7d explains that the catalytic reduction of MB is a first-order kinetic reaction. The rate constant of the methylene blue reduction reaction is 4.6 x 10−2.
(1) Log (A0/A) = kt -------------------
Where A0 is the initial dye concentration and A is the methylene blue concentration at various times t, k the rate constant.
Reduction reaction of methylene blue in the presence of NaBH4 + SMCDs

The study adopted one-pot and cost-effective microwave methods to synthesise carbon dots from Swietenia macrophylla seeds. The average size of the particles was about 4.8 nm, with a quantum yield of about 9.56 %. SMCDs participated in various application processes such as metal sensing, catalysts and antibacterial agents. SMCDs show high selection and sensitivity to detect Fe3+ ions in the aqueous medium, with a limit of detection was about 2.3 μM. SMCDs enhance the rate of the reaction in the methylene blue reduction process/decolourisation. The results concluded that the SMCDs possess a bright blue fluorescent nature with good optical properties and can be used in multifaceted applications.

Hosimin Selvaraj: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Kumaresan Kalainilavan: Formal analysis. Kaleeswaran Sneka Sri: Formal analysis. Hema Sindhuja George: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Priyadharshini Aravind: Data curation. Kavitha Thangavel: Resources. Andivelu Ilangovan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. Hosimin Selvaraj acknowledges UGC for the award of Dr. D. S. Kothari postdoctoral fellowship (DSKPDF) & School of Chemistry, Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli, Tamil Nadu, India, Prof. Andivelu Ilangovan, Kumaresan Kalainilavan and Kaleeswaran Sneka Sri thanks RUSA 2.0 (No.51788/P3/RUSA/2021) for providing funding and facilities at Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620024, Tamil Nadu, India, Dr. Hema Sindhuja George acknowledges the management of Jamal Mohamed College, Tiruchirappalli 620020, Tamil Nadu. India.
